content:
6 min read
Just now

--

The database concept everyone over-explains, and the three things about it that actually matter

Quick question: how many rows do you think your database can search through without an index before things start to feel slow?
A thousand? Ten thousand?
Try this: with a well-built index, a database can find one row out of a billion in roughly the same number of steps it takes to find one row out of a thousand. Not because the computer got faster while you weren’t looking. Because of how the data is organized.
Let’s use one real example and walk through it end to end.
Meet Our Example: The Subscribers Table
Imagine you’re running a small newsletter app. You’ve got a table called subscribers:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Now say your support team constantly runs a query like:
SELECT * FROM subscribers WHERE first_name = 'Joe';With only 7 rows, who cares how this runs. But real tables have millions of rows, and this is exactly the kind of query that separates a snappy app from a support ticket that says “why is search so slow?”
Approach A: No Index (The Full Scan)
Without an index, the database has exactly one option: read every single row, in order, and check each one. This is called a full table scan.
For our 7-row table, that’s 7 checks to find “Joe.” Fine. But scale that table to 10 million subscribers, and finding “Joe” might mean checking all 10 million rows because the database has no way of knowing where the “Joe”s are hiding without looking at everyone.
Approach B: With an Index (The Sorted Shortcut)
An index is just a separate, sorted copy of one column’s values, each pointing back to its row. Think of it like the index in the back of a textbook: instead of reading every page to find “photosynthesis,” you look it up alphabetically and jump straight to page 214.
For our table, an index on first_name looks like this, a plain sorted list:
Jane → row 1
Jane → row 2
Jane → row 3
Jane → row 4
Joe → row 5
Joe → row 6
Lucas → row 7That alone already helps: sorted data means you can jump to the middle, check if you overshot or undershot, and narrow in the same way you’d flip toward the back of a phone book for “Joe” and not start at “Aaron.”
But the real trick is that the database keeps summary layers on top of this sorted list, like signposts. A summary might say “everything in this section starts between Jane and Jane,” or “this section covers Jane through Lucas.” So instead of scanning the sorted list top to bottom, the database hops through a few of these summary signposts and lands almost directly on “Joe” checking a handful of signposts instead of thousands of rows.
This layered, sorted structure is technically called a B+ tree. You don’t need to know how it rebalances itself or splits nodes internally the database handles all of that. What matters is the shape of what it gives you: a sorted list, with shortcuts on top.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

That last row is important. Indexes aren’t free and that’s where things get interesting.
What Happens When Your Data Changes?
Here’s the part most explanations skip: your index isn’t a snapshot, it’s a living structure that updates itself every time the table changes. You don’t write any code for this the database does it automatically but it’s worth knowing what it’s doing under the hood, because it explains a lot of real-world performance behavior.
Let’s run three operations against our subscribers table and see what happens to the first_name index.
1. A row is added. Someone named “Maria” signs up.
INSERT INTO subscribers (first_name, signup_date) VALUES ('Maria', '2024-05-01');The index doesn’t just tack “Maria” onto the end it slots her into the correct sorted position, between “Lucas” and wherever “M” belongs. One new entry, inserted in order.
2. A row is removed. Joe (row 5) unsubscribes and gets deleted.
DELETE FROM subscribers WHERE id = 5;The index entry pointing to row 5 is removed. Simple.
3. A row is changed but only if the indexed column changes. Suppose one of the “Jane”s got her name corrected to “Janet”:
UPDATE subscribers SET first_name = 'Janet' WHERE id = 3;Here’s the subtlety: the database doesn’t “edit” the index entry in place. It removes the old entry and inserts a brand-new one in the correct sorted position for “Janet.” Two operations disguised as one.
But and this is the part people miss if you instead updated signup_date (a column that isn't indexed), the first_name index doesn't do anything at all. It only cares about columns it's actually tracking.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This is why people say “indexes speed up reads but slow down writes.” Every insert, delete, or update to an indexed column means the database is doing extra bookkeeping behind the scenes. With one index, that overhead is trivial. With ten indexes on a hot table, every single write starts paying that tax ten times over.
Sorted vs. Unsorted: Where You Insert Matters
One more wrinkle. That sorted list is optimized to accept new entries anywhere but slotting something into the middle takes more work than adding it to the end.
Picture two ways of generating the id column for new subscribers:

• Sequential IDs (1, 2, 3, 4…): every new subscriber’s ID is bigger than the last, so it always gets appended to the end of the index. Cheap, fast, no shuffling.

• Random IDs (like a random UUID): the new ID could sort anywherebeginning, middle, end forcing the database to insert it into the middle of an already-sorted structure, which takes more work to keep everything sorted correctly.
Tradeoffs: When Should You Actually Think About This?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The honest answer for most developers, most of the time: create the index and move on. The scenarios where insert-order or index-count genuinely bite you only show up at a scale most projects never reach.
The Bigger Lesson
Here’s the thing worth carrying with you past this specific example: a lot of “automatic” systems have a hidden cost model, even when you never have to touch the lever. The database will manage your indexes perfectly well without you writing a line of extra code but the shape of your data and how you write to it still quietly determines how expensive that automation is.
You don’t need to master the internals of every system you rely on. But you do need to know what it’s optimizing for and what it’s trading away because that’s the difference between confidently ignoring a performance concern because it genuinely doesn’t apply to you, versus ignoring it and finding out the hard way at 2am when your “instant” table suddenly isn’t.
